stories.title: Big Jon and Sparkie
stories.author: Jon Arthur
stories.summary: Big Jon and his elf friend Sparkie share stories, songs, and adventures — a Saturday morning children's show from the golden age of radio.
stories.description: No School Today (also known as Big Jon and Sparkie) was an ABC children's program starring Jon Arthur and a child-voiced mechanical elf named Sparkie. The show featured stories, music, and the ongoing serial 'General Comet of the Universe Patrol.' Pure Saturday-morning joy, designed specifically for children ages 4–12.
stories.authorBio: Jon 'Big Jon' Arthur was an American radio personality who hosted No School Today on ABC from 1950 to 1958. The show was one of the last great children's radio programs before television took over.
stories.relevance: Warm, gentle, and made for children — the radio equivalent of a favorite uncle who always has a story.

stories.title: Big Jon and Sparkie: Episode 2
stories.author: ABC Radio
stories.summary: Another episode of Big Jon and Sparkie — the children's radio variety show featuring stories, songs, and the adventures of Big Jon Arthur and his magical friend Sparkie.
stories.description: Big Jon and Sparkie (1950–1958) was an ABC Radio children's program hosted by Jon Arthur ('Big Jon') and featuring his puppet companion Sparkie. The show mixed storytelling, music, and gentle humor in a format designed for young listeners. It was warm, wholesome, and enormously popular with children of the 1950s.
stories.authorBio: Big Jon and Sparkie was an ABC Radio children's variety show (1950–1958) hosted by Jon Arthur. The show's mix of stories, music, and the relationship between Big Jon and his magical elfin companion Sparkie made it a beloved part of many American childhoods.
stories.relevance: Gentle, warm children's radio with stories and music. Big Jon and Sparkie represents the best of 1950s children's programming — wholesome without being dull, entertaining without being loud.

stories.title: The Cinnamon Bear: Paddy O'Cinnamon
stories.author: Glan Heisch
stories.summary: Twins Judy and Jimmy discover a tiny bear named Paddy O'Cinnamon who takes them on a magical Christmas adventure.
stories.description: It's nearly Christmas, and twins Judy and Jimmy climb to the attic to find the silver star for the tree — but it's missing. A toy cinnamon bear named Paddy O'Cinnamon comes to life and tells them the Crazy Quilt Dragon has stolen it. Together they shrink to toy-size and set off on a magical journey to get it back. Originally broadcast in 1937, this 26-part radio serial was a holiday tradition for decades.
stories.authorBio: Glan Heisch wrote and directed The Cinnamon Bear in 1937 for radio broadcast. The serial ran daily from Thanksgiving to Christmas and became one of the most beloved holiday radio programs in American history.
stories.relevance: A perfect Advent listen — one short episode per night from Thanksgiving to Christmas. Gentle enough for young children, magical enough for older ones.

series.name: The Cinnamon Bear
series.nextTeaser: Next: Judy, Jimmy, and Paddy meet Weary Willie on their quest for the silver star.

stories.title: The Cinnamon Bear: Weary Willie
stories.author: Glan Heisch
stories.summary: The children and Paddy O'Cinnamon encounter Weary Willie on their journey through Maybeland.
stories.description: Continuing their quest to recover the silver star from the Crazy Quilt Dragon, Judy, Jimmy, and Paddy travel through Maybeland and meet Weary Willie. Each 15-minute episode introduces a new character and a new challenge, building toward Christmas Day.
stories.authorBio: Glan Heisch wrote and directed The Cinnamon Bear in 1937 for radio broadcast.
stories.relevance: Short, self-contained episodes that end on gentle cliffhangers — ideal for one-per-night bedtime listening.

stories.title: The Greatest Story Ever Told: The Good Samaritan
stories.author: Fulton Oursler
stories.summary: The parable of the Good Samaritan — dramatized with reverence.
stories.description: The Greatest Story Ever Told was an ABC radio program (1947–1956) dramatizing the life of Jesus Christ, based on Fulton Oursler's bestselling book. These are reverent, beautifully produced dramatizations of the Gospels — the kind of thing that used to be on the radio on Sunday evenings.
stories.authorBio: Fulton Oursler (1893–1952) was an American journalist and author whose 1949 book The Greatest Story Ever Told became one of the best-selling religious books of the twentieth century.
stories.relevance: Gospel stories dramatized for the ear — a beautiful way to hear Scripture at bedtime.

stories.title: The Greatest Story Ever Told: The Lost Sheep
stories.author: ABC Radio
stories.summary: Jesus tells the parable of the shepherd who leaves his ninety-nine sheep to search for the one that is lost — dramatized by the award-winning ABC Radio program The Greatest Story Ever Told.
stories.description: The Greatest Story Ever Told was an ABC Radio program (1947–1957) that dramatized episodes from the life of Christ and the parables. The productions are reverent, well-acted, and bring Scripture to life as living drama. The Lost Sheep is one of the most beloved parables — about a God who pursues the one who is lost.
stories.authorBio: The Greatest Story Ever Told (1947–1957) was an ABC Radio program that dramatized Biblical stories with professional actors, original music, and careful attention to Scripture. It was sponsored by the Goodyear Tire and Rubber Company and produced by Henry Denker.
stories.relevance: Scripture brought to life as audio drama. The parable of the Lost Sheep teaches that every single person matters to God — a truth that children need to hear and that this production communicates beautifully.

stories.title: Let's Pretend: Beauty and the Beast
stories.author: CBS Radio
stories.summary: Beauty agrees to live with the Beast to save her father, and discovers that true love sees past appearances — a fairy tale dramatized by the CBS Radio Workshop's beloved children's program.
stories.description: Let's Pretend was a CBS Radio program that aired from 1934 to 1954, dramatizing fairy tales and children's stories with a full cast, original music, and lavish sound effects. It was one of the most beloved children's programs of the Golden Age of Radio, and its productions remain charming, well-acted, and genuinely entertaining.
stories.authorBio: Let's Pretend (1934–1954) was a CBS Radio program created by Nila Mack that dramatized fairy tales and children's stories. It won a Peabody Award in 1943 and was consistently rated among the best children's programs on radio. The show featured child actors, many of whom went on to significant careers.
stories.relevance: Professional radio dramatizations of classic fairy tales — complete with music, sound effects, and a full cast. These are fairy tales experienced as theater, and they bring the stories to life in a way that readings alone cannot.

stories.title: Let's Pretend: Bluebeard
stories.author: CBS Radio
stories.summary: A fairy tale about a mysterious husband and a forbidden room — dramatized with music and young actors.
stories.description: Let's Pretend was CBS's beloved Saturday morning children's program, running from the 1930s through 1954. Young professional actors dramatized classic fairy tales with full orchestral music. These are the original radio performances — warm, vivid, and made with genuine care for young listeners.
stories.authorBio: Let's Pretend (originally The Adventures of Helen and Mary) was created by Nila Mack for CBS Radio. It won the Peabody Award and was considered the gold standard for children's radio drama.
stories.relevance: Radio drama for bedtime — like a movie for the ears. Children who listen to these build their imagination muscles.

stories.title: The Lone Ranger
stories.author: Fran Striker
stories.summary: A masked rider and his loyal companion Tonto bring justice to the Old West — with silver bullets and a moral code.
stories.description: The Lone Ranger was one of the most popular children's radio programs ever broadcast. The masked hero never shot to kill, always left a silver bullet as his calling card, and stood for fairness, justice, and the idea that one person could make a difference. Created at WXYZ Detroit in 1933, it taught virtue to a generation of American children.
stories.authorBio: Fran Striker (1903–1962) created The Lone Ranger for WXYZ radio in Detroit. He wrote over 150 stories for the show and established the Lone Ranger's strict moral code — including the rule that the hero never removes his mask.
stories.relevance: Adventure with a moral compass. The Lone Ranger taught children that strength and gentleness go together — that the strongest person in the room is the one who doesn't need to prove it.

stories.title: Challenge of the Yukon
stories.author: Fran Striker
stories.summary: Sergeant Preston of the Northwest Mounted Police and his lead sled dog Yukon King solve crimes in the frozen north.
stories.description: Challenge of the Yukon (later Sergeant Preston of the Yukon) was a radio adventure series set in the Canadian wilderness. Preston and his dog Yukon King patrol the Yukon during the Gold Rush, delivering justice in the last frontier. Created by the same team behind The Lone Ranger, it has the same strong moral backbone — with the added beauty of wilderness, sled dogs, and the northern lights.
stories.authorBio: Fran Striker and George Trendle created Challenge of the Yukon at WXYZ Detroit — the same station that gave the world The Lone Ranger and The Green Hornet.
stories.relevance: Adventure in the wild — dogs, snow, and justice. For children who love animals and the outdoors.

stories.title: The Adventures of Superman
stories.author: Jerry Siegel & Joe Shuster
stories.summary: A baby from a dying planet is raised by kind farmers in Kansas — and grows up to fight for truth, justice, and the American way.
stories.description: The original Adventures of Superman radio serial, starring Bud Collyer. These are the stories that introduced Superman to millions of American children in the 1940s — heroic, earnest, and powered by the idea that strength should be used to help the weak. The radio Superman is simpler and more purely good than later versions.
stories.authorBio: Superman was created by Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster, two Jewish-American teenagers from Cleveland, Ohio. He first appeared in Action Comics #1 in June 1938 and has been the template for the superhero ever since.
stories.relevance: The original superhero — a story about using power for good. The radio episodes are short, exciting, and end on cliffhangers that make children want to come back tomorrow night.

stories.title: The Ant and the Grasshopper
stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: The ant works all summer while the grasshopper sings — and winter comes for both.
stories.description: One of Aesop's most enduring fables, retold for children. These short stories have taught virtue to children for over two thousand years — not by lecturing, but by showing what happens when animals (who behave very much like people) make choices.
stories.authorBio: Aesop is the legendary author of the world's most famous collection of fables. Whether he was a real person — possibly a Greek storyteller living in the 6th century BCE — or a name attached to a tradition of folk wisdom, the fables themselves have shaped moral teaching for more than two thousand years.
stories.relevance: Aesop's fables are usually a child's first taste of stories that come with a quiet lesson tucked inside. They're short enough for a tired parent and a tireder child.

stories.title: The Boy Who Cried Wolf
stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: A shepherd boy lies about wolves so often that no one believes him when a real wolf comes.

stories.title: The Fox and the Grapes
stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: A short fable about a fox who decides the grapes he cannot reach must be sour anyway.
stories.description: One of Aesop's shortest and most enduring fables. A hungry fox spies a bunch of ripe grapes hanging from a vine, leaps for them again and again, and finally walks away muttering that they were probably sour to begin with. From this little story we get the everyday phrase 'sour grapes' — the way we dismiss the things we cannot have.
stories.authorBio: Aesop is the legendary author of the world's most famous collection of fables. Whether he was a real person — possibly a Greek storyteller and slave living in the 6th century BCE — or a name later attached to a tradition of folk wisdom, the fables themselves have shaped storytelling for more than two thousand years.
stories.relevance: Aesop's fables are usually a child's first taste of stories that come with a quiet lesson tucked inside. They're short enough for a tired parent and a tireder child, and they leave room to talk afterward.

stories.title: The Blue Fairy Book
stories.author: Andrew Lang
stories.summary: The first and most famous of Andrew Lang's twelve colour Fairy Books — fairy tales from around the world.
stories.description: Andrew Lang's Blue Fairy Book, published in 1889, was the first of twelve colour-coded collections that gathered fairy tales from every culture in the world. This first volume includes versions of Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Hansel and Gretel, Aladdin, and many others — drawn from French, German, Norse, and Arabian sources. It is the starting point for one of the great anthologies of children's literature.
stories.authorBio: Andrew Lang (1844–1912) was a Scottish poet, novelist, and literary critic who compiled the twelve Fairy Books between 1889 and 1910. They remain the most comprehensive collection of world fairy tales for young readers in English.
stories.relevance: The greatest fairy tale anthology ever compiled — and the first volume is the best place to start.

stories.title: The Story of Joan of Arc
stories.author: Andrew Lang
stories.summary: A peasant girl from Domrémy hears voices telling her to save France — and she does.
stories.description: Andrew Lang's retelling of the life of Joan of Arc for young readers. Lang was one of the great Victorian collectors of fairy tales, and he brings the same narrative skill to Joan's story — the voices, the Dauphin, the siege of Orléans, the betrayal, and the fire. It reads like a fairy tale because Joan's life was stranger than any fairy tale.
stories.authorBio: Andrew Lang (1844–1912) was a Scottish poet, novelist, and literary critic best known for his twelve coloured Fairy Books. He was also a serious historian who wrote biographies of Joan of Arc, Mary Queen of Scots, and John Knox.
stories.relevance: One of the great saint stories for children — a girl their own age who heard God and obeyed, and who changed the history of a nation.

series.name: The Story of Joan of Arc

stories.title: The Story of Joan of Arc
stories.author: Andrew Lang
stories.summary: Joan hears the voices of the saints and begins her mission — traveling to the Dauphin's court to tell him that God has sent her to save France.
stories.description: Andrew Lang's retelling of the life of St. Joan of Arc, written for young readers. Joan's story is one of the most extraordinary in all of history — a teenage girl who led armies, crowned a king, and was martyred for her faith. Lang tells it with clarity and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Andrew Lang (1844–1912) was a Scottish poet, novelist, and collector of fairy tales. His twelve 'Coloured' Fairy Books gathered stories from traditions around the world. He also wrote popular retellings of historical figures, including Joan of Arc.
stories.relevance: The story of a young woman whose courage, faith, and obedience to God changed the course of history. A saint's story told as the adventure it truly was.

series.name: The Story of Joan of Arc

stories.title: Black Beauty
stories.author: Anna Sewell
stories.summary: A beautiful black horse with a white star on his forehead remembers his first home — a large pleasant meadow with a clear pond.
stories.description: The first chapter of Black Beauty — told in the horse's own voice. A gentle, virtuous animal remembers the kindness of his first master and his mother's advice to always do his best. Sewell wrote it to teach compassion for animals, and it has been teaching it ever since.
stories.authorBio: Anna Sewell (1820–1878) was an English novelist who wrote only one book — Black Beauty — in the last years of her life, while housebound by illness. It became one of the best-selling novels of all time and changed the way people treated horses.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.

stories.title: Wee Ones' Bible Stories
stories.author: Anonymous
stories.summary: The great stories of the Bible told simply for the very youngest children.
stories.description: Bible stories retold in the simplest possible language for toddlers and very young children. The Creation, Noah's Ark, David and Goliath, and the Christmas story — told with warmth and without complexity. Perfect for the youngest bedtime listeners.
stories.authorBio: Anonymous compiled these Bible stories for young readers in the early twentieth century.
stories.relevance: Scripture told as story — the way it was meant to be heard at bedtime.

stories.title: Wee Ones' Bible Stories
stories.author: Anonymous
stories.summary: The great stories of the Bible told simply for the very youngest children.
stories.description: Bible stories retold in the simplest possible language for toddlers and very young children. The Creation, Noah's Ark, David and Goliath, and the Christmas story — told with warmth and without complexity. Perfect for the youngest bedtime listeners.
stories.authorBio: Anonymous compiled these Bible stories for young readers in the early twentieth century.
stories.relevance: Scripture told as story — the way it was meant to be heard at bedtime.

stories.title: Old Peter's Russian Tales
stories.author: Arthur Ransome
stories.summary: Old Peter tells his grandchildren a tale by the fire — of Baba Yaga, the firebird, and the grey wolf.
stories.description: From Old Peter's Russian Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Arthur Ransome (1884–1967) was an English writer and journalist who lived in Russia during the Revolution. He collected these folk tales from Russian peasants and retold them as stories told by an old grandfather to his grandchildren.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.

stories.title: Old Peter's Russian Tales
stories.author: Arthur Ransome
stories.summary: Old Peter tells another tale from the long Russian winter — of frost kings, enchanted animals, and the rewards of kindness.
stories.description: The second tale from Arthur Ransome's collection. Old Peter is a grandfather who tells stories by the fire to his two grandchildren, Vanya and Maroosia. The frame story is as warm as the tales themselves — a family gathered around a stove in the Russian snow.
stories.authorBio: Arthur Ransome (1884–1967) was an English author and journalist best known for his Swallows and Amazons series. Old Peter's Russian Tales, published in 1916, gathers stories he heard during his years in Russia — fairy tales of Baba Yaga, frost kings, and magical fish.
stories.relevance: Russian fairy tales have a special quality — they are wilder and more unpredictable than Western European stories, but they reward the same virtues: kindness, patience, and courage.

stories.title: East of the Sun and West of the Moon
stories.author: Peter Christen Asbjornsen and Jorgen Moe
stories.summary: A girl rides a great white bear to a castle, loses him through curiosity, and must travel east of the sun and west of the moon to win him back.
stories.description: A poor farmer's youngest daughter agrees to go with a great white bear in exchange for her family's fortune. She lives in luxury but is warned never to light a candle at night. When curiosity gets the better of her, she discovers the bear is really an enchanted prince — and loses him. She must then journey to the end of the world to find the castle that lies east of the sun and west of the moon. One of the finest fairy tales in the Scandinavian tradition, rich with wonder and moral weight.
stories.authorBio: Peter Christen Asbjornsen (1812–1885) and Jorgen Moe (1813–1882) were Norwegian folklorists who collected and published Norwegian fairy tales, much as the Grimm brothers did for Germany.
stories.relevance: A sweeping fairy tale with a strong, determined heroine. The Norse atmosphere of trolls, enchanted castles, and northern winds makes for a mesmerizing bedtime listen.

series.name: East of the Sun and West of the Moon
series.nextTeaser: Next: The Three Billy Goats Gruff — the famous tale of the troll under the bridge.

stories.title: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz: The Cyclone
stories.author: L. Frank Baum
stories.summary: A Kansas cyclone carries Dorothy and Toto to the magical Land of Oz.
stories.description: Dorothy lives on the grey Kansas prairie with Uncle Henry and Auntie Em. When a cyclone strikes, she and Toto are carried inside the farmhouse to a strange and colorful land. Baum's opening chapter is one of the most iconic in children's literature — the grey world giving way to vivid color, and an ordinary girl stepping into an extraordinary adventure.
stories.authorBio: L. Frank Baum (1856–1919) was an American author who wrote The Wonderful Wizard of Oz in 1900, intending it as a modern American fairy tale without the frightening elements of European folklore.
stories.relevance: The opening of Oz is pure magic — short enough for a single bedtime session and ending with the perfect cliffhanger of arrival in a new world.

series.name: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz
series.nextTeaser: Next: Dorothy meets the Munchkins and the Good Witch of the North.

stories.title: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz: The Cowardly Lion
stories.author: L. Frank Baum
stories.summary: Dorothy and her friends meet a lion who is afraid of everything — and he joins their journey.
stories.description: Deep in the forest, a great lion leaps onto the road and tries to frighten Dorothy's companions. But when Dorothy slaps his nose and calls him a coward, the Lion breaks down in tears. He confesses he is terrified of everything, and Dorothy invites him to come to the Emerald City where the Wizard might give him courage. Baum's message — that admitting fear is the first step toward bravery — resonates with every child who has ever been afraid at bedtime.
stories.authorBio: L. Frank Baum (1856–1919) was an American author who wrote The Wonderful Wizard of Oz in 1900.
stories.relevance: The Cowardly Lion's honesty about his fears makes this the most relatable chapter for children. A natural bedtime conversation starter about being brave.

series.name: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz
series.nextTeaser: Next: The journey through the dark forest to the Emerald City.

stories.title: The Tale of Benjamin Bunny
stories.author: Beatrix Potter
stories.summary: Peter Rabbit's cousin Benjamin Bunny convinces Peter to return to Mr. McGregor's garden to retrieve the clothes he lost — but Mr. McGregor's cat is waiting.
stories.description: From The Tale of Peter Rabbit and Others. Beatrix Potter's animal stories are marvels of compression — each one is a complete adventure in just a few pages, told in precise, witty prose with Potter's own watercolor illustrations in mind. The stories are gentle but never saccharine; Peter really could have been put in a pie.
stories.authorBio: Beatrix Potter (1866–1943) was an English writer and illustrator best known for her children's books featuring animal characters. The Tale of Peter Rabbit, first published in 1902, has sold over 45 million copies. Her stories are short, precise, and perfect for the youngest listeners.
stories.relevance: The gold standard for bedtime stories for the youngest listeners. Each tale is short enough to finish in one sitting, vivid enough to see with your eyes closed, and funny enough to make a parent smile too.

stories.title: The Tale of Jemima Puddle-Duck
stories.author: Beatrix Potter
stories.summary: Jemima Puddle-Duck wants to hatch her own eggs, but the charming gentleman fox she meets in the woods has other plans entirely.
stories.description: From The Tale of Peter Rabbit and Others. Beatrix Potter's animal stories are marvels of compression — each one is a complete adventure in just a few pages, told in precise, witty prose with Potter's own watercolor illustrations in mind. The stories are gentle but never saccharine; Peter really could have been put in a pie.
stories.authorBio: Beatrix Potter (1866–1943) was an English writer and illustrator best known for her children's books featuring animal characters. The Tale of Peter Rabbit, first published in 1902, has sold over 45 million copies. Her stories are short, precise, and perfect for the youngest listeners.
stories.relevance: The gold standard for bedtime stories for the youngest listeners. Each tale is short enough to finish in one sitting, vivid enough to see with your eyes closed, and funny enough to make a parent smile too.

stories.title: The Tale of Mrs. Tiggy-Winkle
stories.author: Beatrix Potter
stories.summary: Little Lucie has lost her pocket handkerchiefs and follows a path up the hill to the house of Mrs. Tiggy-Winkle, a very stout, short hedgehog who takes in washing.
stories.description: From The Tale of Peter Rabbit and Others. Beatrix Potter's animal stories are marvels of compression — each one is a complete adventure in just a few pages, told in precise, witty prose with Potter's own watercolor illustrations in mind. The stories are gentle but never saccharine; Peter really could have been put in a pie.
stories.authorBio: Beatrix Potter (1866–1943) was an English writer and illustrator best known for her children's books featuring animal characters. The Tale of Peter Rabbit, first published in 1902, has sold over 45 million copies. Her stories are short, precise, and perfect for the youngest listeners.
stories.relevance: The gold standard for bedtime stories for the youngest listeners. Each tale is short enough to finish in one sitting, vivid enough to see with your eyes closed, and funny enough to make a parent smile too.

stories.title: The Tale of Peter Rabbit
stories.author: Beatrix Potter
stories.summary: Naughty Peter Rabbit disobeys his mother and sneaks into Mr. McGregor's garden, where he eats too many vegetables and very nearly ends up in a pie.
stories.description: From The Tale of Peter Rabbit and Others. Beatrix Potter's animal stories are marvels of compression — each one is a complete adventure in just a few pages, told in precise, witty prose with Potter's own watercolor illustrations in mind. The stories are gentle but never saccharine; Peter really could have been put in a pie.
stories.authorBio: Beatrix Potter (1866–1943) was an English writer and illustrator best known for her children's books featuring animal characters. The Tale of Peter Rabbit, first published in 1902, has sold over 45 million copies. Her stories are short, precise, and perfect for the youngest listeners.
stories.relevance: The gold standard for bedtime stories for the youngest listeners. Each tale is short enough to finish in one sitting, vivid enough to see with your eyes closed, and funny enough to make a parent smile too.

stories.title: Cinderella
stories.author: Brothers Grimm
stories.summary: A girl in ashes is given a golden dress by the birds in her mother's tree — and the prince finds her at the ball.
stories.description: From the Brothers Grimm's Household Tales — the stories that European families told their children for centuries before the Grimms wrote them down. These are not the sanitized versions; they are the real tales, with real consequences and real rewards.
stories.authorBio: Jacob (1785–1863) and Wilhelm (1786–1859) Grimm were German academics and linguists who collected and published folk tales from oral tradition. Their Household Tales preserved stories that had been told in European homes for centuries — Cinderella, Snow White, Hansel and Gretel, Rapunzel — and shaped the fairy tale tradition as we know it.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told the way they were told around the fire. Short, vivid, and full of the kind of justice children understand instinctively.

stories.title: Hansel and Gretel
stories.author: Brothers Grimm
stories.summary: Two children lost in the forest find a house made of bread and sugar — and the old woman inside is not what she seems.
stories.description: From the Brothers Grimm's Household Tales — the stories that European families told their children for centuries before the Grimms wrote them down. These are not the sanitized versions; they are the real tales, with real consequences and real rewards.
stories.authorBio: Jacob (1785–1863) and Wilhelm (1786–1859) Grimm were German academics and linguists who collected and published folk tales from oral tradition. Their Household Tales preserved stories that had been told in European homes for centuries — Cinderella, Snow White, Hansel and Gretel, Rapunzel — and shaped the fairy tale tradition as we know it.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told the way they were told around the fire. Short, vivid, and full of the kind of justice children understand instinctively.

stories.title: Rapunzel
stories.author: Brothers Grimm
stories.summary: A girl locked in a tower lets down her hair — and a prince climbs it.
stories.description: From the Brothers Grimm's Household Tales — the stories that European families told their children for centuries before the Grimms wrote them down. These are not the sanitized versions; they are the real tales, with real consequences and real rewards.
stories.authorBio: Jacob (1785–1863) and Wilhelm (1786–1859) Grimm were German academics and linguists who collected and published folk tales from oral tradition. Their Household Tales preserved stories that had been told in European homes for centuries — Cinderella, Snow White, Hansel and Gretel, Rapunzel — and shaped the fairy tale tradition as we know it.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told the way they were told around the fire. Short, vivid, and full of the kind of justice children understand instinctively.

stories.title: Snow White
stories.author: Brothers Grimm
stories.summary: A queen asks her mirror who is the fairest — and the mirror answers honestly.
stories.description: From the Brothers Grimm's Household Tales — the stories that European families told their children for centuries before the Grimms wrote them down. These are not the sanitized versions; they are the real tales, with real consequences and real rewards.
stories.authorBio: Jacob (1785–1863) and Wilhelm (1786–1859) Grimm were German academics and linguists who collected and published folk tales from oral tradition. Their Household Tales preserved stories that had been told in European homes for centuries — Cinderella, Snow White, Hansel and Gretel, Rapunzel — and shaped the fairy tale tradition as we know it.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told the way they were told around the fire. Short, vivid, and full of the kind of justice children understand instinctively.

stories.title: The Adventures of Pinocchio
stories.author: Carlo Collodi
stories.summary: A carpenter finds a piece of wood that laughs and cries — and carves it into a puppet who immediately runs away.
stories.description: The real Pinocchio is wilder and stranger than the Disney version. A puppet who lies, runs from home, and gets into terrible trouble — but who keeps being given chances to become real. Collodi's original is a Catholic morality tale about the long, painful, funny process of becoming fully human.
stories.authorBio: Carlo Collodi (1826–1890) was the pen name of Carlo Lorenzini, a Florentine journalist and children's author. He wrote Pinocchio as a serial for a children's newspaper in 1881, and it became Italy's most famous children's book.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.

stories.title: The Adventures of Pinocchio
stories.author: Carlo Collodi
stories.summary: Geppetto carves the puppet and names him Pinocchio — and as soon as his legs are finished, Pinocchio runs away into the street, beginning a long career of trouble.
stories.description: The second chapter of Collodi's masterpiece. The original Pinocchio is wilder, funnier, and more dangerous than any adaptation — a wooden puppet who is selfish, reckless, and constantly running from the consequences of his lies. His journey toward becoming a real boy is the original redemption story for children.
stories.authorBio: Carlo Collodi was the pen name of Carlo Lorenzini (1826–1890), an Italian author best known for The Adventures of Pinocchio. First serialized in a children's magazine in 1881, it is the story of a wooden puppet who must learn honesty, obedience, and selflessness before he can become a real boy.
stories.relevance: Pinocchio's story is fundamentally about conscience, obedience, and the hard road from selfishness to love. The puppet must learn to tell the truth, keep his promises, and put others before himself — only then does he become real.

stories.title: The Heroes
stories.author: Charles Kingsley
stories.summary: Perseus sets out to slay the Gorgon Medusa — guided by Athena and armed with winged sandals and a cap of darkness.
stories.description: From The Heroes — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Charles Kingsley (1819–1875) was an English clergyman, novelist, and social reformer who retold the Greek myths of Perseus, the Argonauts, and Theseus specifically for his own children — making them the first great English versions intended for young readers.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.

stories.title: The Water-Babies
stories.author: Charles Kingsley
stories.summary: Tom the chimney sweep falls into a river and becomes a water-baby — beginning a journey of purification and wonder.
stories.description: Kingsley's great Christian allegory for children. Tom is a chimney sweep — dirty, abused, and ignorant. He falls into a river and is transformed into a tiny water-baby, beginning a journey through underwater worlds where he must learn to be clean inside and out. It is a baptismal story, a nature story, and a social protest rolled into one strange, beautiful book.
stories.authorBio: Charles Kingsley (1819–1875) was an English clergyman, novelist, and social reformer. He wrote The Water-Babies in 1863 as both a fairy tale for his youngest son and a protest against child labor.
stories.relevance: A journey story for bedtime — transformation, wonder, and the idea that everyone deserves to be made clean.

stories.title: Cinderella
stories.author: Charles Perrault
stories.summary: A girl in cinders is given one night of beauty by her fairy godmother — and leaves behind a glass slipper.
stories.description: The original Cinderella, as Perrault told it in 1697. Not the Disney version — gentler in some ways, sharper in others. The pumpkin coach, the glass slipper, and the stroke of midnight are all here, but so is Perrault's dry wit and his Catholic sense that goodness is its own reward.
stories.authorBio: Charles Perrault (1628–1703) was a French author and member of the Académie française who established the modern fairy tale genre. A devout Catholic, he published Tales of Mother Goose in 1697, giving literary form to Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Puss in Boots, and Little Red Riding Hood.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told by a devout Catholic Frenchman in 1697. These are the versions that shaped every retelling since.

stories.title: Puss in Boots
stories.author: Charles Perrault
stories.summary: A clever cat inherits nothing but his wits and talks his way to a kingdom for his master.
stories.description: The most delightfully amoral of Perrault's tales — a cat who lies, cheats, and bluffs his way to a fortune. Children love it for the cleverness; parents can use it to talk about when cunning is and isn't a virtue.
stories.authorBio: Charles Perrault (1628–1703) was a French author and member of the Académie française who established the modern fairy tale genre. A devout Catholic, he published Tales of Mother Goose in 1697, giving literary form to Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Puss in Boots, and Little Red Riding Hood.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told by a devout Catholic Frenchman in 1697. These are the versions that shaped every retelling since.

stories.title: Sleeping Beauty
stories.author: Charles Perrault
stories.summary: A princess pricks her finger on a spindle and sleeps for a hundred years, waiting for the prince brave enough to wake her.
stories.description: Perrault's Sleeping Beauty is longer and stranger than the version most people know. The hundred-year sleep, the enchanted castle, and the prince who arrives to find roses growing over the walls — it is one of the great images of patience and hope in fairy tale literature.
stories.authorBio: Charles Perrault (1628–1703) was a French author and member of the Académie française who established the modern fairy tale genre. A devout Catholic, he published Tales of Mother Goose in 1697, giving literary form to Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Puss in Boots, and Little Red Riding Hood.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told by a devout Catholic Frenchman in 1697. These are the versions that shaped every retelling since.

stories.title: Sing-Song: A Nursery Rhyme Book
stories.author: Christina Rossetti
stories.summary: Gentle nursery rhymes by Christina Rossetti — about stars, babies, kittens, and the coming of sleep.
stories.description: Christina Rossetti's Sing-Song is a collection of 126 nursery rhymes written with a poet's ear and a Christian devotional sensibility. They are about simple things — wind, rain, babies, animals, sleep — and they carry the same quiet depth as her adult poetry. Perfect for the very youngest listeners.
stories.authorBio: Christina Rossetti (1830–1894) was an English poet and devout Anglican whose work includes Goblin Market, In the Bleak Midwinter, and this collection of nursery rhymes. She wrote with precision, beauty, and a deep religious faith.
stories.relevance: Nursery rhymes by a great poet — the kind of thing that sounds simple and stays in memory forever.

stories.title: Sing-Song: A Nursery Rhyme Book
stories.author: Christina Rossetti
stories.summary: More of Rossetti's tender nursery verses — about kittens, caterpillars, the wind, the moon, and the gentle rhythm of a child's day from morning to bedtime.
stories.description: The second selection from Christina Rossetti's Sing-Song. These are poems written with a mother's tenderness and a poet's ear — short, musical, and full of the natural world. They are among the finest nursery rhymes in the English language.
stories.authorBio: Christina Rossetti (1830–1894) was an English poet associated with the Pre-Raphaelite movement. A devout Anglican, her Sing-Song: A Nursery Rhyme Book is a collection of tender, musical verses for the very young — about lambs, babies, wind, and sleep.
stories.relevance: Perfect lullaby-length poems for the youngest listeners. Rossetti's verses have the same soothing, rhythmic quality as a rocking chair — gentle repetition, soft images, natural cadence.

stories.title: A Christmas Carol: Marley's Ghost
stories.author: Charles Dickens
stories.summary: The ghost of Jacob Marley visits his old partner Scrooge on Christmas Eve, dragging chains of greed.
stories.description: Ebenezer Scrooge is the meanest man in London. On Christmas Eve, his dead partner Jacob Marley appears as a ghost, bound in heavy chains forged from a lifetime of selfishness. Marley warns Scrooge that three spirits will visit him — and that this is his last chance to change. Dickens wrote this in just six weeks in 1843, and it became the most famous Christmas story ever told.
stories.authorBio: Charles Dickens (1812–1870) was an English novelist whose works include Oliver Twist, David Copperfield, and Great Expectations. A Christmas Carol was written partly as social commentary on poverty in Victorian England.
stories.relevance: A timeless Christmas story about redemption. Best suited for older children who can handle the ghostly atmosphere — the payoff is one of the most joyful endings in literature.

series.name: A Christmas Carol
series.nextTeaser: Next: The Ghost of Christmas Past takes Scrooge on a journey through his memories.

stories.title: Five Children and It
stories.author: E. Nesbit
stories.summary: Five children dig in a gravel pit and find a Psammead — a sand-fairy who grants wishes that expire at sunset.
stories.description: The first chapter of one of the great children's fantasies. Five siblings move to a house in the country, go exploring, and dig up a furry, bat-eared creature who has been sleeping in the sand since the dinosaurs. He grants one wish per day, each of which goes spectacularly wrong. Nesbit invented the template for every 'be careful what you wish for' children's story that followed.
stories.authorBio: E. Nesbit (1858–1924) was an English author and co-founder of the Fabian Society who wrote some of the most inventive children's novels of her era. Five Children and It, The Railway Children, and The Enchanted Castle blend everyday family life with magic in a way that felt entirely new and still feels fresh.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.

stories.title: Five Children and It
stories.author: E. Nesbit
stories.summary: The children make their first wish — to be 'as beautiful as the day' — and discover that wishes granted by a sand-fairy don't work out quite the way you expect.
stories.description: The second chapter of E. Nesbit's comic masterpiece. The Psammead grants one wish per day, and each wish goes spectacularly wrong. It is the template for every 'be careful what you wish for' story that came after.
stories.authorBio: E. Nesbit (1858–1924) was an English author and co-founder of the Fabian Society who wrote some of the most inventive children's novels of her era. Five Children and It, The Railway Children, and The Enchanted Castle blend everyday family life with magic in a way that felt entirely new and still feels fresh.
stories.relevance: A funny, cautionary story about getting exactly what you asked for. Each chapter's wish is a self-contained comic disaster — perfect bedtime episodes.

stories.title: The Railway Children
stories.author: E. Nesbit
stories.summary: Three children — Roberta, Peter, and Phyllis — move from London to a cottage near a railway line when their father mysteriously goes away. The railway becomes their world, and the trains carry more than passengers.
stories.description: E. Nesbit's most beloved realistic novel. The Railway Children is about a family dealing with loss and uncertainty while maintaining courage, humor, and kindness. The children's adventures on the railway — waving to trains, preventing disasters, befriending the station porter — are both exciting and deeply moving.
stories.authorBio: E. Nesbit (1858–1924) was an English author and co-founder of the Fabian Society who wrote some of the most inventive children's novels of her era. Five Children and It, The Railway Children, and The Enchanted Castle blend everyday family life with magic in a way that felt entirely new and still feels fresh.
stories.relevance: A story about courage, loyalty, and the strength of family in difficult times. Roberta's quiet determination to hold her family together is one of the most moving portrayals of childhood responsibility in children's literature.

stories.title: Nonsense Verses
stories.author: Edward Lear
stories.summary: The Owl and the Pussycat, the Jumblies, the Dong with the Luminous Nose, and other gloriously absurd poems from the master of English nonsense.
stories.description: Edward Lear's nonsense poetry is pure delight — musical, absurd, and irresistible to children. His poems create entire worlds out of invented words, impossible creatures, and journeys to nowhere. They are the English language at play.
stories.authorBio: Edward Lear (1812–1888) was an English artist and poet known for his literary nonsense, especially his limericks and poems like 'The Owl and the Pussycat.' He brought sheer delight and absurdity to children's verse, and his work has been in continuous print for over 150 years.
stories.relevance: Pure fun. Lear's poems are the antidote to overly earnest children's literature — they exist to make children laugh, to delight in the sound of words, and to prove that nonsense is a perfectly serious business.

stories.title: Jataka Tales
stories.author: Ellen C. Babbitt
stories.summary: A monkey king sacrifices himself to save his troop — one of the ancient Jataka tales of wisdom and virtue.
stories.description: From Jataka Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Ellen C. Babbitt (1872–?) was an American educator who retold the Buddhist Jataka birth stories for children in 1912. Each tale has a clear moral, and the stories feature animals as the Buddha's past lives — very similar in structure to Aesop's fables.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.

stories.title: Jataka Tales
stories.author: Ellen C. Babbitt
stories.summary: Another animal fable from ancient India — teaching cleverness, generosity, and the wisdom of thinking before you act.
stories.description: The second tale from Ellen C. Babbitt's retelling of the Jataka Tales. These ancient Buddhist stories — over 2,000 years old — use animal characters to teach moral lessons that are universal: be kind, be clever, think of others, and don't be fooled by flattery.
stories.authorBio: Ellen C. Babbitt (1878–1946) was an American author who retold the Jataka Tales — ancient Buddhist stories about the previous lives of the Buddha — for young Western readers. The tales teach kindness, cleverness, and generosity through animal fables.
stories.relevance: Some of the oldest moral tales in the world, retold for young readers. The Jataka Tales predate Aesop and share the same teaching-through-story tradition.

stories.title: Black Folk Tales
stories.author: Erick Berry
stories.summary: Hausa folk tales from Nigeria — short, clever stories of animals and wisdom.
stories.description: From Black Folk Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Erick Berry (pen name of Evangeline Champlin, 1892–1974) lived in West Africa in the 1920s and retold Hausa folk tales from Nigeria. Short, engaging stories with animal characters and moral outcomes.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.

stories.title: The Biography of a Grizzly
stories.author: Ernest Thompson Seton
stories.summary: Wahb the grizzly bear loses his mother and brothers on the first page — and spends a lifetime growing into the king of the mountains.
stories.description: From The Biography of a Grizzly — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Ernest Thompson Seton tells the life story of Wahb, a grizzly bear in the Yellowstone country — from orphaned cub to the greatest bear in the mountains.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.

stories.title: Wild Animals I Have Known
stories.author: Ernest Thompson Seton
stories.summary: Lobo, the king of the Currumpaw wolves, outsmarts every trap — until the hunters find what he loves.
stories.description: From Wild Animals I Have Known — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Ernest Thompson Seton (1860–1946) was a British-Canadian naturalist and author who helped found the Boy Scouts of America. His animal stories treat their subjects as individuals with dignity and courage.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.

stories.title: Bannertail: The Story of a Graysquirrel
stories.author: Ernest Thompson Seton
stories.summary: A young gray squirrel, orphaned and raised by a kind human, must learn to survive in the wild — finding food, avoiding hawks and cats, and discovering the ancient ways of his kind.
stories.description: Seton's most charming animal biography. Bannertail is a real character — curious, brave, and resourceful — and his adventures in the woods are based on Seton's own careful observations of gray squirrels over many years.
stories.authorBio: Ernest Thompson Seton (1860–1946) was a British-born Canadian-American naturalist, author, and illustrator who co-founded the Boy Scouts of America. His animal stories — drawn from real observation — combine scientific accuracy with narrative power. He believed that children who understood wild animals would grow into better people.
stories.relevance: A nature story grounded in real observation. Seton makes the reader care about a squirrel the way they'd care about a person — because he understood that every wild animal has a story.

stories.title: Titus: A Comrade of the Cross
stories.author: Florence Morse Kingsley
stories.summary: A young Roman encounters the early Christians — and discovers a faith that changes everything.
stories.description: Florence Morse Kingsley's vivid historical fiction set in the time of the early Church. Titus is a young Roman who encounters the followers of Christ and finds himself drawn into a community of courage, sacrifice, and radical love. For older children ready for stories about what it cost to be a Christian when being a Christian could cost your life.
stories.authorBio: Florence Morse Kingsley (1859–1937) was an American author who wrote a series of popular historical novels about the early Church. Titus, Stephen, and Paul follow characters through the first century of Christianity.
stories.relevance: Historical fiction with a Christian spine — for the child who is ready to hear what faith looked like when it was dangerous.

series.name: Titus: A Comrade of the Cross

stories.title: A Little Princess
stories.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
stories.summary: Sara Crewe arrives at Miss Minchin's boarding school as the richest pupil — and imagines herself a princess.
stories.description: The first chapter of Burnett's great novel about imagination and dignity. Sara Crewe has everything — and then loses everything — and discovers that being a princess is not about what you have but about how you behave when you have nothing. One of the great stories about grace under pressure.
stories.authorBio: Frances Hodgson Burnett (1849–1924) was an English-American novelist who also wrote A Little Princess and Little Lord Fauntleroy. The moors and walled gardens of her childhood Manchester thread through The Secret Garden, written in 1911.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.

stories.title: The Secret Garden
stories.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
stories.summary: Mary Lennox, sour and lonely, is sent from India to live with her uncle on the Yorkshire moors.
stories.description: Chapter 1 — 'There Is No One Left.' A cholera outbreak in colonial India leaves nine-year-old Mary Lennox an orphan, alone in a great empty bungalow until soldiers come and find her hiding behind a curtain. The chapter is quiet and a little sad and very precise about her loneliness. She is dispatched across the world to a great, draughty house on the Yorkshire moors belonging to a hunchbacked uncle she has never met — and the rest of the book begins from there. One of the great quiet openings in children's literature.
stories.authorBio: Frances Hodgson Burnett (1849–1924) was an English-American novelist who also wrote A Little Princess and Little Lord Fauntleroy. She moved between England and the United States all her life, and the moors and walled gardens of her childhood Manchester thread through The Secret Garden, written in 1911.
stories.relevance: A long, slow, healing book — about gardens and weather and a child who is allowed to be cross. Pairs well with rainy nights and tea.

series.name: The Secret Garden
series.nextTeaser: Mary arrives at Misselthwaite Manor and meets the brisk Mrs Medlock — and on her first morning, hears a strange sound she's been told is only the wind.

stories.title: The Secret Garden
stories.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
stories.summary: Mary meets Martha, the Yorkshire maid who tells her about the locked garden, the crying in the night, and the moor where everything grows wild and free.
stories.description: Frances Hodgson Burnett's most enduring novel — the story of a sour, lonely girl who discovers a hidden garden and, in bringing it back to life, brings herself and others back to life too. The Secret Garden is about the healing power of nature, friendship, and the stubborn persistence of growing things.
stories.authorBio: Frances Hodgson Burnett (1849–1924) was an English-American novelist who also wrote A Little Princess and Little Lord Fauntleroy. The moors and walled gardens of her childhood Manchester thread through The Secret Garden, written in 1911.
stories.relevance: A slow, beautiful story about transformation — Mary's character unfolds as gradually as the garden itself. Each chapter reveals a little more of the mystery and a little more of Mary's thawing heart.

stories.title: The Secret Garden
stories.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
stories.summary: Mary begins to explore the grounds and meets Ben Weatherstaff, the old gardener, and a friendly robin who seems to want to show her something hidden behind the ivy.
stories.description: Frances Hodgson Burnett's most enduring novel — the story of a sour, lonely girl who discovers a hidden garden and, in bringing it back to life, brings herself and others back to life too. The Secret Garden is about the healing power of nature, friendship, and the stubborn persistence of growing things.
stories.authorBio: Frances Hodgson Burnett (1849–1924) was an English-American novelist who also wrote A Little Princess and Little Lord Fauntleroy. The moors and walled gardens of her childhood Manchester thread through The Secret Garden, written in 1911.
stories.relevance: A slow, beautiful story about transformation — Mary's character unfolds as gradually as the garden itself. Each chapter reveals a little more of the mystery and a little more of Mary's thawing heart.

stories.title: Baby
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Tender poems about the wonder of a new baby — written for the youngest listeners.
stories.description: A short poem by George MacDonald, the great Scottish Christian fantasist. MacDonald's poems for children have the same luminous quality as his fairy tales — simple on the surface, deep underneath.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: Short enough for the smallest children, beautiful enough for the parent reading aloud. MacDonald's poems are lullabies in everything but name.

stories.title: Bedtime
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: A gentle poem about the coming of sleep and the peace of the night.
stories.description: A short poem by George MacDonald, the great Scottish Christian fantasist. MacDonald's poems for children have the same luminous quality as his fairy tales — simple on the surface, deep underneath.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: Short enough for the smallest children, beautiful enough for the parent reading aloud. MacDonald's poems are lullabies in everything but name.

stories.title: The Day Boy and the Night Girl
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: A wicked witch raises two children in opposite conditions — one who has never seen the night, and one who has never seen the day. When they finally meet, everything changes.
stories.description: Also known as Photogen and Nycteris. A boy raised only in sunlight and a girl raised only in moonlight must discover each other — and the full world — to break the witch's power. It is one of MacDonald's most beautiful shorter tales, about complementarity and wholeness.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A story about how no one is complete alone — the day boy needs the night girl, and she needs him. MacDonald weaves theology into fairy tale so gently that children absorb it as wonder.

stories.title: The Gifts of the Child Christ
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: On Christmas night, a neglected little girl named Sophy receives a gift that no one else can see — and through it, begins to mend her broken family.
stories.description: MacDonald's most explicitly Christian short story. A cold household is warmed by the faith of a small child and the mysterious workings of grace at Christmas. It is tender without being sentimental, and its vision of Christmas cuts deeper than any Dickens.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A Christmas story in the deepest sense — about what the coming of the Christ Child actually means for a family. Perfect for Advent and Christmas bedtime listening.

stories.title: The Light Princess
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: A princess cursed to have no gravity floats through life laughing — but she has lost the ability to cry.
stories.description: A wicked aunt curses a newborn princess to have no gravity — she floats to the ceiling, bounces off walls, and laughs at everything because she cannot take anything seriously. It sounds funny, and it is, but MacDonald is also writing about what it means to have no weight in the world — no sorrow, no depth, no love that costs anything. The cure, when it comes, costs everything.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: Funny enough for young children, deep enough for parents to think about afterward. MacDonald at his most playful and his most serious at the same time.

stories.title: The Light Princess
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: The princess discovers the lake, the one place where she feels weight, and a prince discovers her there.
stories.description: A wicked aunt curses a newborn princess to have no gravity — she floats to the ceiling, bounces off walls, and laughs at everything because she cannot take anything seriously. It sounds funny, and it is, but MacDonald is also writing about what it means to have no weight in the world — no sorrow, no depth, no love that costs anything. The cure, when it comes, costs everything.

stories.relevance: Funny enough for young children, deep enough for parents to think about afterward. MacDonald at his most playful and his most serious at the same time.

stories.title: The Light Princess and Other Fairy Tales
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: A collection of MacDonald's shorter fairy tales, beginning with the story of a princess cursed to have no gravity — she floats through life laughing, unable to take anything seriously, until love teaches her weight.
stories.description: This collection gathers several of MacDonald's shorter fairy tales into one volume. The stories share MacDonald's signature blend of playfulness and depth — princesses, enchantments, and moral transformation told with a poet's ear and a minister's heart.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A sampler of MacDonald's fairy-tale genius. The stories are self-contained and varied enough to sustain many bedtimes.

stories.title: The Lost Princess
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Two girls — a spoiled princess named Rosamond and a wild shepherd's daughter named Agnes — are both lost in a great forest, where a wise woman begins the hard work of teaching them who they really are.
stories.description: Also known as A Double Story or The Wise Woman's Telling. Two girls from opposite ends of society are equally in need of correction — one spoiled by privilege, the other by neglect. MacDonald's story is about the painful, necessary work of becoming good, told through the language of fairy tale.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A fairy tale about character formation — the kind of slow, honest growth that MacDonald believed was the real adventure. Perfect for children old enough to understand that being lost can be the beginning of being found.

stories.title: At the Back of the North Wind
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Little Diamond meets the North Wind — a beautiful, terrible woman who carries him through the night sky.
stories.description: A coachman's son named Diamond lives in a hayloft above the stable. One night the North Wind comes through a crack in the wall and takes him flying over London. She is beautiful and frightening and kind, and Diamond comes to love her. This is MacDonald's most mystical children's book — a story about death and heaven told so gently that children hear it as a fairy tale and adults hear it as theology.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: One of the great bedtime books — literally about a child being carried through the night. The prose has a dreamy, lullaby quality that suits the hour.

series.name: At the Back of the North Wind
series.nextTeaser: Diamond rides with North Wind across London and begins to understand that she is not always gentle.

stories.summary: Diamond rides with North Wind across London and begins to understand that she is not always gentle.

stories.title: The Princess and Curdie
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Curdie, now a young miner, is losing his sense of wonder — until the great-great-grandmother summons him for a new mission, and gives him a terrifying gift: the ability to feel the true nature of anyone's hand.
stories.description: The sequel to The Princess and the Goblin. Curdie is older, harder, beginning to doubt his childhood experiences. The grandmother gives him a quest to save the king and the gift of discernment — when he shakes someone's hand, he feels the animal they are becoming. It is MacDonald's darkest and most profound children's book.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A story about growing up without growing cynical. The gift of the grandmother's fire — the ability to see through surfaces to the truth of a person — is one of the great images in children's literature.

stories.title: The Princess and the Goblin
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Princess Irene discovers a mysterious staircase in her mountain home and meets her beautiful great-great-grandmother.
stories.description: George MacDonald's most beloved children's story — a tale of a princess, a miner's boy, and the goblins beneath the mountain. It is a book about courage and trust, about following a thread you cannot see, and about the kind of grandmother who lives at the top of a tower and spins moonlight. MacDonald wrote it for children but meant it for everyone. C.S. Lewis called it a book that had 'baptised his imagination.'
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A slow, luminous fairy tale that rewards being read chapter by chapter at bedtime. The grandmother's thread is one of the great images in children's literature — a child following something she trusts through the dark.

series.name: The Princess and the Goblin
series.nextTeaser: Curdie the miner's boy hears the goblins singing beneath the mountain — and their words are not kind.
